critique.summary: The article describes 'Get Shit Done' (GSD), a system designed to facilitate meta-prompting, context engineering, and spec-driven development primarily for AI models like Claude Code. It emphasizes simplifying complex workflows, maintaining context quality, and automating code generation and verification processes.
mainPoints: GSD aims to solve context degradation ('context rot') in AI workflows by managing context effectively.
It provides a structured, XML-based prompt format and orchestrates multi-agent processes for research, planning, execution, and verification.
Designed for solo developers and small teams, GSD emphasizes simplicity, reliability, and avoiding enterprise complexity.
The system supports multiple AI models and runtimes, with commands for project initialization, phase management, and automation.
GSD promotes atomic commits, modular workflows, and detailed context management to ensure consistent and traceable development.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a detailed overview of GSD's features and design philosophy, with claims about solving context degradation and automating development workflows. These claims align with known challenges in AI prompt engineering and context management, and the system's architecture appears plausible based on existing AI development practices. However, as it is a product description, independent verification of its effectiveness or real-world performance is limited. The references to trusted companies like Amazon, Google, Shopify, and Webflow suggest some credibility, but specific independent evaluations or user testimonials are not provided.
considerations: Lack of independent third-party validation or empirical results demonstrating GSD's effectiveness.
Potential limitations in handling very large or complex projects beyond small teams or solo developers.
The system's reliance on specific AI models and prompt formatting may require technical expertise to implement effectively.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around various AI-driven development frameworks like GSD, Superpowers, openspec, and PAUL, with users sharing experiences, comparisons, and critiques on their effectiveness, usability, and practicality in real-world projects.

comments.author: gtirloni
comments.text: I was using this and superpowers but eventually, Plan mode became enough and I prefer to steer Claude Code myself. These frameworks are great for fire-and-forget tasks, especially when there is some research involved but they burn 10x more tokens, in my experience. I was always hitting the Max plan limits for no discernable benefit in the outcomes I was getting. But this will vary a lot depending on how people prefer to work.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view based on personal experience, highlighting the trade-offs and limitations of these frameworks, making it quite credible.

comments.author: DamienB
comments.text:
I've compared this to superpowers and the classic prd->task generator. And I came away convinced that less is more. At least at the moment. gsd performed well, but took hours instead of minutes. 
Having a simple explanation of how to create a PRD followed by a slightly more technical task list performed much better. It wasn't that grd or superpowers couldn't find a solution, it's just that they did it much slower and with a lot more help. 
For me, the lesson was that the workflow has changed, and we that we can't apply old project-dev paradigms to this new/alien technology. 
There's a new instruction manual and it doesn't build on the old one.
comments.analysis: The critique emphasizes efficiency and workflow changes, supported by personal testing, which adds credibility to the evaluation.

comments.author: maccam912
comments.text: I've had a good experience with https://github.com/obra/superpowers. At first glance this looks similar. Has anyone tried both who can offer a comparison?
comments.analysis: This comment seeks comparative insights based on personal use, which is valuable but lacks detailed evidence.

comments.author: gbrindisi
comments.text:
I like openspec, it lets you tune the workflow to your liking and doesn’t get in the way.

I started with all the standard spec flow and as I got more confident and opinionated I simplified it to my liking.

I think the point of any spec driven framework is that you want to eventually own the workflow yourself, so that you can constraint code generation on your own terms.
comments.analysis: The user shares a positive, experience-based perspective on openspec's flexibility, adding practical insight.

comments.author: theodorewiles
comments.text: I think the research / plan / execute idea is good but feels like you would be outsourcing your thinking. Gotta review the plan and spend your own thinking tokens!
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid concern about the potential over-reliance on automated planning, emphasizing the importance of human oversight.

comments.author: arjie
comments.text: I could not produce useful output from this. It was useful as a rubber duck because it asks good motivating questions during the plan phase, but the actual implementation was lacklustre and not worth the effort. In the end, I just have Claude Opus create plans, and then I have it write them to memory and update it as it goes along and the output is better.
comments.analysis: The critique is subjective and somewhat dismissive, but it highlights the perceived shortcomings in practical output.

comments.author: galexyending
comments.text: I gave it a shot, but won't be using it going forward. It requires a waterfall process. And, I found it difficult, and in some cases impossible, to adjust phases/plans when bugs or changes in features arise. The execution prompts didn't do a good job of steering the code to be verified while coding and relies on the user to manually test at the end of each phase.
comments.analysis: This comment points out practical limitations related to flexibility and adaptability in real projects.

comments.author: dfltr
comments.text: GSD has a reputation for being a token burner compared to something like Superpowers. Has that changed lately? Always open to revisiting things as they improve.
comments.analysis: This is a general inquiry without specific evidence, making it less reliable as a critique.

comments.author: dhorthy
comments.text:
it is very hard for me to take seriously any system that is not proven for shipping production code in complex codebases that have been around for a while.

I've been down the "don't read the code" path and I can say it leads nowhere good.

I am perhaps talking my own book here, but I'd like to see more tools that brag about "shipped N real features to production" or "solved Y problem in large-10-year-old-codebase"

I'm not saying that coding agents can't do these things and such tools don't exist, I'm just afraid that counting 100k+ LOC that the author didn't read kind of fuels the "this is all hype-slop" argument rather than helping people discover the ways that coding agents can solve real and valuable problems.
comments.analysis: The skepticism about new tools' readiness for production is valid but somewhat conservative, lacking specific evidence.
